elements.sent-2411-001: To address communication latency issues, the Third Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) has defined Cellular-Vehicle to Everything (C-V2X) technology, which includes Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) communication for direct vehicle-to-vehicle communication.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, this method requires vehicles to autonomously select communication resources based on the Semi-Persistent Scheduling (SPS) protocol, which may lead to collisions due to different vehicles sharing the same communication resources, thereby affecting communication effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-003: Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA) is considered a potential solution for handling large-scale vehicle communication, as it can enhance the Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR) by employing Successive Interference Cancellation (SIC), thereby reducing the negative impact of communication collisions.
elements.sent-2411-004: When evaluating vehicle communication performance, traditional metrics such as reliability and transmission delay present certain contradictions.
elements.sent-2411-005: Introducing the new metric Age of Information (AoI) provides a more comprehensive evaluation of communication system.
elements.sent-2411-006: Additionally, to ensure service quality, user terminals need to possess high computational capabilities, which may lead to increased energy consumption, necessitating a trade-off between communication energy consumption and effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-007: Given the complexity and dynamics of communication systems, Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) serves as an intelligent learning method capable of learning optimal strategies in dynamic environments.
elements.sent-2411-008: Therefore, this paper analyzes the effects of multi-priority queues and NOMA on AoI in the C-V2X vehicular communication system and proposes an energy consumption and AoI optimization method based on DRL.
elements.sent-2411-009: Finally, through comparative simulations with baseline methods, the proposed approach demonstrates its advances in terms of energy consumption and AoI.

elements.sent-2411-012: The traditional approach requires vehicles to first communicate with base stations, then accessing data stored in data centers through the access core backbone network, and finally, the data center returns the requested data\cite{wang2021cooperative,wang2010delay,fan2002investigation, w18 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: It is evident that this method experiences long end-to-end delays and has limited bandwidth for data return\cite{dai2019artificial,wu2023delay, w17, w19 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: C-V2X is defined by 3GPP, with "V" representing vehicles and "X" indicating communication with other vehicles, network infrastructure, road infrastructure, pedestrians, and so on\cite{silva2017survey,shao2024semantic .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The issue of long end-to-end delays is addressed by V2V communication within this framework.
elements.sent-2411-016: In C-V2X mode 4, resources are autonomously selected by vehicular user equipment without reliance on cellular infrastructure, thus resolving issues of excessive delay and overhead\cite{molina2017lte,wang2024vehicle, w23 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In mode 4, the automatic selection of communication resources by vehicles is termed as SPS, which is based on a distributed sensing-based semi-persistent resource scheduling protocol\cite{nabil2018performance, w25 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Semi-persistence is exemplified by vehicles being able to choose one of the standardized Resource Reservation Intervals (RRI) and then determining the corresponding number of communication slots.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, this method may lead to collision risks as the same resources may be reserved by vehicles\cite{dayal2021adaptive,ji2024graph, w22 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: NOMA is a potential solution to address collision risks in C-V2X communication.
elements.sent-2411-021: It is a promising technology for handling large-scale vehicle communication \cite{Deng2023,yue2024hybrid,w24 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: NOMA can help alleviate performance degradation in terms of latency and packet reception probability caused by high vehicle density\cite{di2017non .}
elements.sent-2411-023: SIC is currently a detection technique can decode signals from multiple users occupying the same resource by leveraging their different signal strengths, thereby reducing interference in the SINR and minimizing latency, thus mitigating the impact of collisions\cite{zhang2017sic .}
elements.sent-2411-024: In communication systems, the environment can be influenced by various factors such as changes in user numbers, fluctuations of channel conditions, and the presence of interference\cite{wu2023characterizing,wu2022characterizing,w28,w29 .}

elements.sent-2411-029: In existing engineering and 3GPP standards, AoI is the latest metric for measuring communication effectiveness\cite{leng2019AoI,qi2024deep .}
elements.sent-2411-030: This is because lower delay or higher reliability both result in lower average AoI, indicating better timeliness of communication\cite{yates2021age .}
elements.sent-2411-031: AoI refers to the time interval between the current time and the generation time of the data to be transmitted or received.
elements.sent-2411-032: If the average AoI at the receiving end is large, it means that the received data was generated a long time ago, indicating poor timeliness of communication.

elements.sent-2411-087: Fig. \ref{fig1 shows a C-V2X vehicular networking system.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Assuming a highway divided into two lanes in each direction, with vehicles randomly distributed and moving at uniform speeds.
elements.sent-2411-089: Vehicles closer to the center lane travel faster, mimicking real highway scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-090: Each vehicle considers other vehicles within a circular area of radius $w$ as potential receivers.
elements.sent-2411-091: Each vehicle maintains a message queue to store newly generated but unprocessed message data, with a maximum queue length of $L$, following the policy of FCFS.
elements.sent-2411-092: If the number of messages in the queue reaches $L$, newly generated messages are discarded due to overflow.
elements.sent-2411-093: Each vehicle in C-V2X utilizes the SPS scheme to reserve communication resources for itself.
elements.sent-2411-094: When new messages enter the message queue, vehicles check if they have any reserved communication resources.
elements.sent-2411-095: If not, they establish a Selection Window (SW) at that moment.
elements.sent-2411-096: The SW serves as a time window, indicating the duration within which the vehicle can reserve resources for itself in the future.
elements.sent-2411-097: This time window, along with its corresponding frequency domain, forms a time-frequency multiplexing structure capable of providing communication resources, known as the resource pool.
elements.sent-2411-098: Vehicles, after partitioning the resource pool, assess the occupancy of resources in the previous one thousand subframes based on metrics like Reference Signal Received Power (RSRP) and Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI) to determine suitable resources for communication.
elements.sent-2411-099: They then randomly reserve some of these resources for future use.
elements.sent-2411-100: Vehicles determine the RRI based on the size of the SW, i.e., the duration of the resource pool.
elements.sent-2411-101: Additionally, vehicles calculate the number of times they can use the resources according to the protocol and store it as a Reselection Counter (RC).
elements.sent-2411-102: Each time the resources are used for transmission, the RC is decremented until it reaches zero, at which point the vehicle waits for the process to repeat when a new message enters the queue.
elements.sent-2411-103: This resource selection method can lead to overlapping SW when multiple vehicles determine their SW at roughly similar times, resulting in collisions where they reserve the same resource for future broadcast communication.
elements.sent-2411-104: When many vehicles simultaneously occupy the same resource, then the vehicles as receivers use SIC-based NOMA to decode messages in descending order of power.
elements.sent-2411-105: Lower-power messages are treated as interference and excluded after decoding the highest power message, thereby improving the SINR of each message and reducing the AoI resulting from communication failures.
elements.sent-2411-106: AoI reflects the time spent in queuing and transmission processes, where the queuing process is affected by packet generation rate or RRI, and the other process is influenced by success rate or delay.
elements.sent-2411-107: Reducing RRI decreases AoI but increases energy consumption and collision probability, affecting SINR and increasing AoI.
elements.sent-2411-108: Reducing transmission power can decrease energy consumption but also reduces SINR, leading to increased AoI.
elements.sent-2411-109: Hence, there is a need to mitigate collision effects and jointly optimize energy consumption and AoI.
elements.sent-2411-110: An RSU with a coverage radius of $R$ is placed at the roadside, capable of sending resource selection notifications to vehicles entering its coverage area.
elements.sent-2411-111: In each time slot, vehicles check whether resource reselection is needed and select RRI size and transmission power based on information provided by the RSU.
elements.sent-2411-112: Through trained policies, the RSU can choose appropriate actions based on vehicle status to optimize AoI and energy consumption.

elements.section-2411-004: DRL Method for Optimization of RRI and Power Allocation
elements.sent-2411-190: Considering the uncertain channel conditions of the C-V2X system, this section introduces an Multi-Pass deep Q-Networks (MPDQN) method based on DRL to solve the $\Gamma$ and $p$ allocation problems of vehicles in the system.
elements.sent-2411-191: Using the DRL framework to model the problem, which mainly includes states, actions, policies, and rewards.
elements.sent-2411-192: Specifically, the RSU takes action $a^t$ based on the current state $s^t$ and the policy, and obtains the corresponding reward $r^t$ and the state $s^{t+1}$ for the next time slot.
elements.sent-2411-193: State: In time slot $t$, vehicle $i$ uses broadcasting to communicate with other vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-194: The number of receivers for vehicle $i$ is $N^t$, and the average distance between them and vehicle i is $d_i$.
elements.sent-2411-195: When vehicle $i$ transmits messages in time slot $t$, the ratio of the number of successful recipients to the total number is $Rn$, reflecting the impact of current transmission power on the communication process under uncertain channel conditions.
elements.sent-2411-196: Therefore, the state of vehicle $i$ in the time slot is defined as \begin{equation}{s}^t_i = [N^t_i,d^t_i,Rn^t_i,RC^0_i] . \label{state}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-197: Action: RSU allocates transmission intervals and power to vehicles based on their status, therefore the actions of vehicle $i$ in time slots are defined as \begin{equation}{a}^t_i = [{\Gamma}^t_i, {p}^t_i] . \label{action1}\end{equation} The $\Gamma$ is a discrete value and $p$ is a continuous value, so the MPDQN algorithm that can simultaneously make choices for actions in this situation is used to solve it.
elements.sent-2411-198: First, match each discrete action with a continuous action as part of the state, treating the two actions as one action tuple, and then select the action tuple based on the state.
elements.sent-2411-199: Therefore, in MPDQN, the action tuple of a vehicle is defined as \begin{equation}{a}^t_i = ({\Gamma}^t_i, {p}_\Gamma) . \label{action2}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-200: Reward function: In this chapter, the goal of RSU is to optimize communication performance in the system, including AoI and energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-201: Every time a vehicle selects an action, it will use it until the next reserved resource is selected.
elements.sent-2411-202: Therefore, the energy consumption generated by vehicle $i$ during that time period and the AoI of its receiver will be weighted and averaged as the reward function.
elements.sent-2411-203: So the reward function for vehicles in time slot $t$ is defined as \begin{equation}{r}^t_i=-[\omega_{1}E^t_{i}+ \omega_2\overline{\Phi^t_{i}}] ,\end{equation} $\overline{\Phi^t_{i }$ is the average AoI received by vehicle i at the receiving end during this time period, expressed as \begin{equation}\overline {\Phi _i^t} = \frac{1}{T}\frac{1}{{{N_v}}}\sum\limits_{t = 1}^T {\sum\limits_{j \in {N_v}}^{} {\Phi _{i \to j}^t} } .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-204: This means that the action space is not solely discrete or continuous, which makes traditional DRL methods inadequate.
elements.sent-2411-205: Therefore, MPDQN is needed to directly handle this situation.
elements.sent-2411-206: The agent using MPDQN first evaluates the value of each discrete action and corresponding continuous action based on the state and $\theta_x$.
elements.sent-2411-207: It then pairs each discrete action with the continuous action that has the highest corresponding value, forming an action tuple.
elements.sent-2411-208: Finally, it identifies the action tuple with the highest value based on the state and $\theta _Q$, determining the optimal discrete and continuous actions for the current state.
elements.sent-2411-209: According to \cite{MPDQN3,MPDQN4 , for a set of action $({\Gamma ^t_i, {p _\Gamma)$, its state action value function can be expressed as $Q(s^t,a^t) = Q\left( {s^t,\left( {\Gamma ,{p_\Gamma \right) \right)$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-210: To correspond $\Gamma$ and $p$, define a policy network as the transition function between discrete and continuous actions in state $s^t$ \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} p_\Gamma ^t = {x^Q}\left( {{s^t},{\Gamma}^t ;{\theta _x}} \right) \end{aligned} , \label{xQ}\end{equation} where $\theta_x$ is the weight of the network.
elements.sent-2411-211: Therefore, the action value function of vehicle $i$ in state $s^t_i$ and action $({\Gamma ^t_i, {p _\Gamma)$ is \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} Q\left( {s_i^t,\left( {\Gamma _i^t,{p_\Gamma }} \right)} \right) = Q\left( {s_i^t,\left( {\Gamma _i^t,{x^Q}\left( {s_i^t,\Gamma ;{\theta _x}} \right)} \right)} \right) \end{aligned} . \label{Qvaluex}\end{equation} Using a deep neural network with network weight $\theta_Q$ to approximate $Q(s,(\Gamma),p)$, the Eq. \ref{Qvaluex can be written as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} Q(s_i^t,a_i^t) = Q\left( {s_i^t,\left( {\Gamma _i^t,{x^Q}\left( {s_i^t,\Gamma ;{\theta _x}} \right)} \right);{\theta _Q}} \right) \end{aligned} . \label{QvalueQ}\end{equation} Then, by updating the network weights, the optimization objective of the problem is approached, and the target value of the vehicles in the scene is defined as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} y_{}^t = r_{}^t + \gamma \mathop {\max }\limits_\Gamma Q\left( {{s^{t + 1}},{\Gamma ^{t + 1}},{x^Q}\left( {{s^{t + 1}};{\theta _x}} \right);{\theta _Q}} \right) \end{aligned} .\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-212: The loss function of each network is defined as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \begin{array}{l} L_{Q}\left(\theta_{Q}\right)= \underset{\left(s^t, (\Gamma, p_{\Gamma}), r^t, s^{t+1}\right) \sim M}{\mathbb{E}}\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(y^{t}-Q\left(s^t,\left(\Gamma, p_{\Gamma}\right) ; \theta_{Q}\right)\right)^{2}\right] \end{array} \end{aligned} , \label{LossQ}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \begin{array}{l} L_{x}\left(\theta_{x}\right)= \underset{s^t \sim M}{\mathbb{E}}\left[- \underset{{\Gamma=\{20,50,10\}}}\sum Q\left(s^t, \Gamma, x^{Q}\left(s, \Gamma ; \theta_{x}\right) ; \theta_{Q}\right)\right] \end{array} \end{aligned} . \label{Lossx}\end{equation} Finally, update the parameters through the following methods \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \theta _Q^{t + 1} = \theta _Q^t - l{r_Q}{\nabla _{{\theta _Q}}}{L_Q}\left( {{\theta _Q}} \right) \end{aligned} , \label{updateQ}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \theta _x^{t + 1} = \theta _x^t - l{r_x}{\nabla _{{\theta _x}}}{L_x}\left( {{\theta _x}} \right) \end{aligned} , \label{updatex}\end{equation} the $lr_x$ and $lr_Q$ are the learning rates of the two policy networks.
elements.sent-2411-213: Next, we will explain the algorithm in detail, with the pseudocode in Algorithm \ref{al1 .}
elements.sent-2411-214: Firstly, randomly initialize $\theta_x$ and $\theta_Q$, and establish an experience replay buffer $M$.
elements.sent-2411-215: Next, the algorithm loops through $EP$ segments, resetting the system model simulation parameters at the beginning of each segment.
elements.sent-2411-216: The intelligent agent outputs the action tuple $({\Gamma ^t_i, {p _\Gamma)$ based on the initial network parameters, and then observes the state ${s ^1_i = [N^1_i,d^1_i,Rn^1_i,RC^0_i]$ after the action is used.}}}
elements.sent-2411-217: Then, the algorithm cycles from time slot 1 to time slot $T$.
elements.sent-2411-218: For each time slot $t$, it randomly selects with a certain probability or calculates the corresponding $p$ for each $\Gamma$ according to formula Eq. \ref{xQ .}
elements.sent-2411-219: Then, according to Eq. \ref{QvalueQ , the action tuple $({\Gamma ^t_i, {p _\Gamma)$ with the highest $Q$ value is obtained, and exploration noise is added.}}}
elements.sent-2411-220: Finally, the intelligent agent will store the observed reward $r$, state $s^{t+1}$, and previous state actions as tuples $[s^t,(\Gamma^t,p^t),r^t,s^{t+1 ]$ in $M$.}
elements.sent-2411-221: When the number of tuples exceeds the size of the sample $B$, $B$ tuples are taken from it to update the network parameters.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Optimization algorithm based on MPDQN} \label{al1} \KwIn{$\gamma$, $\tau_Q$, $\tau_x$, $\theta_Q$, $\theta_x$} \KwOut{optimized $\theta_Q$, $\theta_x$} Randomly initialize the $\theta_Q$, $\theta_x$\; Initialize replay experience buffer $M$\; \For{episode from $1$ to $EP$ } { Reset simulation parameters for the system model\; Receive initial observation state $\boldsymbol{s}_{0}$\; \For{slot $t$ from $1$ to $T$ } { Generate action tuples\; Execute action $\left( {\Gamma _i^t,{p_\Gamma }} \right)$, observe reward $r^t$ and new state $\boldsymbol{s}^{t+1}$ from the system model\; Store transition tuple $\left[ {{s^t},\left( {{\Gamma ^t},{p_\Gamma }} \right),{r^t},{s^{t + 1}}} \right]$\; \If {number of tuples in $M$ is larger than $B$ } { Randomly sample a mini-batch of $B$ transitions tuples from $M$\; Update the critic network by minimizing the loss function according to Eq. \eqref{LossQ} and Eq. \eqref{Lossx}\; Update the actor network according to Eq. \eqref{updateQ} and Eq. \eqref{updatex}. } } }
elements.sent-2411-222: In this section, we conducted extensive simulation experiments on the scene in Fig. \ref{fig1 , using Python 3.6 and MATLAB 2023b as simulation tools.}
elements.sent-2411-223: Simulation was added and modified based on \cite{cecchini2017ltev2vsim .}
elements.sent-2411-224: Assuming the length of the highway $D$ is $500m$, the number of vehicles $N_v$ is $20-50$, and the communication distance $w$ of the vehicles is $150m$.
elements.sent-2411-225: According to the C-V2X standard, the bandwidth is set as 10 MHz and QPSK modulation for propagation.
elements.sent-2411-226: The $T_C$ of CAM takes $100ms$, and the other three types of messages are $\lambda$ set to 0.0001.
elements.sent-2411-227: The $T_H$, $T_D$, $K_H$ and $K_D$ are set to $100ms$, $500ms$, 8 and 5 respectively.

elements.sent-2411-232: The existing work adopts genetic algorithms and random strategies as baseline algorithms for resource allocation.
elements.sent-2411-233: Therefore, we chose these two algorithms for comparison.
elements.sent-2411-234: The introduction of random strategy and genetic algorithm here is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-235: Random policy: In C-V2X, vehicles randomly allocate power for each vehicle within the range $[0, P_{max ]$ based on the utilization of multiple priority queues and NOMA.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Additionally, each vehicle's RRI is randomly assigned from the set ${20, 50, 100}$.
elements.sent-2411-237: GA-based policy: In each time slot, the RSU randomly generates a population vector based on the range of RRI and power values, as well as the population size, and uses the reward function as the fitness function.
elements.sent-2411-238: Each individual in the population represents the RRI and power allocation for all vehicle.
elements.sent-2411-239: The RSU employs a competition method to select the better individuals from the population vector based on the fitness of all individuals for crossover, generating offspring.
elements.sent-2411-240: These offspring undergo mutation operations and are then provided to the vehicles to obtain new fitness.
elements.sent-2411-241: Through multiple iterations of evolution, the crossover and mutation operations explore better actions, approaching the optimal RRI and power allocation.
elements.figure-2411-006: \subfigtopskip=2pt \subfigbottomskip=2pt \subfigure[Single queue]{ \label{single_low} \includegraphics[scale=0.5]{figure/sin.eps} } \subfigure[Multi-priority queue]{ \label{mul-low} \includegraphics[scale=0.5]{figure/mul.eps} } \caption{The AoI in queue with low processing rates.}
elements.sent-2411-242: Fig. \ref{single_low compares the average AoI variation for different types of messages from single-priority queue vehicles in the scenario when the $\Gamma$ is selected as $100ms$.}
elements.sent-2411-243: It is evident that as the number of vehicles increases, the average AoI of each message type in the queue gradually increases.
elements.sent-2411-244: Moreover, since the processing rate of vehicle message queues is $10/s$ when the $\Gamma=100ms$, and the sum of production rates for all message types is significantly higher than this value, all message types struggle to be transmitted in a timely manner.
elements.sent-2411-245: Therefore, the relative sizes of AoI among these messages are correlated with their respective production rates.
elements.sent-2411-246: Messages with higher production rates exhibit larger AoI values.
elements.sent-2411-247: Consequently, the average AoI of the CAM type, which has the highest production rate, is the largest.
elements.sent-2411-248: While the average AoI of the MHD message type, which has the least frequent production rate, is the smallest.
elements.sent-2411-249: Fig. \ref{mul-low compares the average AoI variations of messages of various types for multi-priority queue vehicles in the scenario when the RRI is 100ms.}
elements.sent-2411-250: It can be observed that the higher the priority of a message, the smaller its average AoI, while the lower the priority, the larger the average AoI, ensuring the freshness of critical information when not all information transmission can be guaranteed in the communication environment.
elements.sent-2411-251: Moreover, compared to various types of messages in a single-priority queue, the average AoI of messages with higher priority becomes smaller.
elements.sent-2411-252: This is because in the presence of priorities, messages with lower priority always need to wait for messages with higher priority to be transmitted first, resulting in a lower processing rate for the queue of lower-priority messages compared to the queue of higher-priority messages.
elements.sent-2411-253: Among them, CAM-type messages show the largest variation because they have the highest message generation frequency, making them more prone to accumulation, especially when all types of messages are in a single queue.
elements.sent-2411-254: Therefore, when the priority of CAM-type messages is higher than that of MHD-type messages, the freshness of lower-priority MHD messages is sacrificed to ensure the transmission of CAM-type messages, thus reducing the average AoI.
